name: FIFA World Cup
description: Every FIFA World Cup since 1930, men's and women's. Who won, who scored the goals that got replayed for decades, who the dynasties are.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the very first FIFA World Cup in 1930?
facts.explanation: Uruguay hosted and won the inaugural World Cup, defeating Argentina 4-2 in the final at the Estadio Centenario in Montevideo. Only 13 teams entered, largely due to a European boycott over travel costs.
facts.wowFactor: Uruguay won the World Cup in their own backyard — 13 nations showed up, most of Europe stayed home.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country hosted the inaugural 1930 FIFA World Cup?
facts.explanation: Uruguay was chosen to host because they were reigning Olympic football champions (1924 and 1928) and agreed to cover travel costs for visiting nations. The tournament was held entirely in Montevideo.
facts.wowFactor: Uruguay paid for everyone's boats to get there — and then beat them all.

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the top scorer at the 1930 FIFA World Cup with 8 goals?
facts.explanation: Argentine striker Guillermo Stábile scored 8 goals in the tournament despite only playing because another player withdrew through injury. He never appeared for the national team in a World Cup again after 1930.
facts.wowFactor: Stábile was a last-minute replacement who then led the tournament in goals — and never played another World Cup.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 1930 World Cup Final between Uruguay and Argentina?
facts.explanation: Uruguay came from behind to win 4-2, with Argentina leading 2-1 at half time. The victory sparked massive celebrations in Uruguay and riots in Buenos Aires.
facts.wowFactor: Argentina led at half time — Uruguay scored 3 in the second half to claim the first-ever World Cup.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1934 FIFA World Cup, defeating Czechoslovakia 2-1 after extra time?
facts.explanation: Italy defeated Czechoslovakia 2-1 after extra time in Rome. It was the first World Cup where the defending champion (Uruguay) had to qualify — and Uruguay refused to enter in protest of the European boycott of 1930.
facts.wowFactor: Italy won under Mussolini's regime, and Uruguay refused to even show up — football's first political flashpoint.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country hosted the 1934 FIFA World Cup?
facts.explanation: Italy hosted the second World Cup under the Fascist government of Benito Mussolini, who used the tournament as a propaganda showcase. Italy won on home soil.
facts.wowFactor: Mussolini personally oversaw the 1934 tournament — politics and football have never been fully separate.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Czechoslovakian player was the top scorer at the 1934 FIFA World Cup with 5 goals?
facts.explanation: Oldřich Nejedlý of Czechoslovakia led the tournament with 5 goals, helping his team to the final where they lost to Italy 2-1 after extra time. He was one of the finest European forwards of the interwar era.
facts.wowFactor: His team reached the final but he is almost forgotten today — a great player erased by time and war.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country retained the FIFA World Cup trophy in 1938, winning back-to-back titles?
facts.explanation: Italy defeated Hungary 4-2 in the final at Colombes, France, to become the first team to retain the World Cup. It would be the last tournament for 12 years — WWII cancelled the 1942 and 1946 editions.
facts.wowFactor: Italy were back-to-back champions before WWII shut down the tournament for 12 years.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian striker led the 1938 World Cup with 7 goals and was nicknamed 'The Black Diamond'?
facts.explanation: Leônidas da Silva (nicknamed 'O Diamante Negro') scored 7 goals in 1938 and is credited with popularizing the bicycle kick. Brazil controversially rested him for the semi-final to save him for a final they never reached.
facts.wowFactor: Brazil rested their top scorer for the semi-final to save him for the final — and then lost the semi-final.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country stunned 173,850 fans in the Maracanã to win the 1950 FIFA World Cup?
facts.explanation: Uruguay defeated Brazil 2-1 in the decisive final match of a round-robin final stage. Brazil only needed a draw to win the title but Uruguay scored twice in the second half. The event became known as the Maracanazo.
facts.wowFactor: The Maracanazo — the quietest 200,000 people ever to fill a stadium, as Uruguay scored the winner.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian striker led the 1950 World Cup with 8 goals, only to see his nation lose the decisive final match?
facts.explanation: Ademir de Menezes scored 8 goals in the tournament to win the top scorer award, but Brazil lost the decisive final round-robin match to Uruguay 2-1, creating the 'Maracanazo' heartbreak.
facts.wowFactor: Ademir was the best scorer of the tournament — playing for the team that suffered football's greatest upset on home soil.

facts.quizQuestion: In which stadium did the 1950 World Cup's decisive final match take place, before the largest crowd in World Cup history?
facts.explanation: The Maracanã Stadium in Rio de Janeiro held an estimated 173,850 fans for Uruguay vs Brazil — the largest verified attendance in World Cup history. Brazil lost 2-1 in one of football's greatest upsets.
facts.wowFactor: The Maracanã holds 200,000 but was never the same after what happened on July 16, 1950.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won the 1954 World Cup in the 'Miracle of Bern', defeating the previously unbeaten Hungarian 'Golden Team'?
facts.explanation: West Germany defeated Hungary 3-2 in the final despite Hungary having beaten them 8-3 in the group stage. The 'Wunderteam' of Hungary were considered unbeatable — they hadn't lost in 4 years.
facts.wowFactor: Hungary beat West Germany 8-3 in the group stage. Three weeks later, West Germany won the final.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Hungarian player scored 11 goals at the 1954 World Cup — still the second-highest tally for a single tournament?
facts.explanation: Sándor Kocsis ('Golden Head') scored 11 goals in the 1954 tournament, including two hat-tricks. Only Just Fontaine's 13 in 1958 beats this record. Despite leading the scoring, Hungary lost the final to West Germany.
facts.wowFactor: Kocsis scored 11 in a single tournament and still ended up on the losing side in the final.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1958 World Cup in Sweden, featuring the debut of a 17-year-old named Pelé?
facts.explanation: Brazil defeated hosts Sweden 5-2 in the final at Råsunda Stadium. Pelé became the youngest player ever to score in a World Cup final, aged 17 years and 249 days.
facts.wowFactor: A 17-year-old named Pelé cried after the final whistle — then went on to win two more.

facts.quizQuestion: Which French striker scored 13 goals at the 1958 World Cup — the all-time single-tournament record?
facts.explanation: Just Fontaine scored 13 goals in just 6 matches at the 1958 World Cup in Sweden, averaging more than 2 goals per game. This record has stood for over 65 years and is considered one of sport's most unbreakable records.
facts.wowFactor: 13 goals in 6 games — Fontaine's 1958 record has stood for more than 65 years and counting.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 1958 World Cup Final between Brazil and Sweden?
facts.explanation: Brazil demolished hosts Sweden 5-2 in Solna. Pelé scored twice, and Vavá scored twice as well. The match announced Brazil as the world's pre-eminent football nation.
facts.wowFactor: Brazil 5-2 Sweden — the day a teenager named Pelé introduced himself to the world.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country retained the World Cup trophy in 1962, despite Pelé's early injury ending his tournament?
facts.explanation: Brazil defeated Czechoslovakia 3-1 in the Santiago final. Pelé was injured in the second group match and played no further part — Garrincha stepped up to carry the team to the title.
facts.wowFactor: Pelé got injured in the second game. Garrincha won it for Brazil anyway.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did the top scorers of the 1962 World Cup each score? (Six players shared the lead.)
facts.explanation: The 1962 World Cup had the most players tied for top scorer — six players each scored 6 goals: Garrincha, Vavá, Dražan Jerković, Leonel Sánchez, Flórián Albert, and Valentin Ivanov. A unique moment in World Cup history.
facts.wowFactor: Six players tied for top scorer in 1962 — the only time the Golden Boot has ever been a six-way draw.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1966 FIFA World Cup on home soil at Wembley Stadium?
facts.explanation: England defeated West Germany 4-2 after extra time at Wembley. Geoff Hurst scored a hat-trick — the only hat-trick in a World Cup final — including a controversial goal that may not have crossed the line.
facts.wowFactor: England's only World Cup — won with a goal that might not have crossed the line. The debate rages 60 years on.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Portuguese striker scored 9 goals at the 1966 World Cup, helping Portugal finish third?
facts.explanation: Eusébio scored 9 goals in 6 games for Portugal at the 1966 World Cup in England, winning the Golden Boot by a wide margin. His goals included 4 in the quarter-final comeback from 3-0 down against North Korea.
facts.wowFactor: Eusébio scored 4 goals in one half to drag Portugal back from 3-0 down against North Korea.

facts.quizQuestion: In which stadium was the 1966 FIFA World Cup Final played, when England beat West Germany 4-2?
facts.explanation: The 1966 final was held at the old Wembley Stadium in London, England's national ground since 1923. The famous 'did it cross the line?' goal — England's third — was confirmed by a Soviet linesman.
facts.wowFactor: A Soviet linesman decided England's greatest sporting moment — the irony was lost on nobody.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1970 World Cup in Mexico with what is widely regarded as the greatest team ever assembled?
facts.explanation: Brazil's 1970 squad — featuring Pelé, Jairzinho, Rivellino, and Tostão — destroyed Italy 4-1 in the final. Jairzinho scored in every match. Brazil kept the Jules Rimet Trophy permanently for winning it three times.
facts.wowFactor: Brazil's 1970 team is still the standard by which all great sides are measured — they won the trophy forever.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 1970 World Cup Final between Brazil and Italy in Mexico City?
facts.explanation: Brazil demolished Italy 4-1 in the Estadio Azteca. Carlos Alberto's final goal — a sweeping team move finished with a thunderous shot — is considered one of the greatest team goals ever scored.
facts.wowFactor: Carlos Alberto's finish to the 1970 final — a team move involving nearly every outfield player — is still the pinnacle of World Cup football.

facts.quizQuestion: Which West German striker scored 10 goals at the 1970 World Cup — the third-highest single-tournament tally ever?
facts.explanation: Gerd Müller scored 10 goals for West Germany in Mexico 1970, winning the Golden Boot by a massive margin. Known as 'Der Bomber', he combined prolific scoring with lethal penalty box instinct.
facts.wowFactor: Gerd Müller scored 10 in a single tournament — and still had Fontaine's 13 above him in the record books.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won the 1974 World Cup in Munich, defeating the Netherlands' revolutionary 'Total Football' side?
facts.explanation: West Germany defeated the Netherlands 2-1 in the final at the Olympiastadion. The Dutch opened the scoring before West Germany had even touched the ball — but lost the final.
facts.wowFactor: Netherlands scored before West Germany touched the ball in the final. West Germany still won.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Polish winger won the 1974 Golden Boot with 7 goals as Poland surprised the world to finish third?
facts.explanation: Grzegorz Lato of Poland scored 7 goals in 1974 and won the Golden Boot as Poland had their best-ever World Cup, finishing third. Johan Cruyff — who many expected to win it — scored 3.
facts.wowFactor: Poland finished third in 1974 — a result so surprising that Lato's Golden Boot is still one of the most unexpected in history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won its first FIFA World Cup on home soil in 1978, defeating the Netherlands in the final?
facts.explanation: Argentina won the 1978 World Cup in Buenos Aires with a 3-1 extra-time victory over the Netherlands. The tournament was held under Argentina's military dictatorship, which used it as propaganda.
facts.wowFactor: Ticker tape rained down in Buenos Aires as Argentina won — while the military junta used the celebration to hide its crimes.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Argentine striker won the 1978 Golden Boot with 6 goals, including two in the final?
facts.explanation: Mario Kempes was the tournament's top scorer with 6 goals and the hero of the final, scoring twice as Argentina defeated the Netherlands 3-1 after extra time. He was the only non-Dutch player on the tournament's team of the tournament.
facts.wowFactor: Kempes scored in the final to win his country's first World Cup — every goal he scored came after halftime.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1982 World Cup in Spain, with Paolo Rossi as both Golden Ball and Golden Boot winner?
facts.explanation: Italy defeated West Germany 3-1 in the final at the Santiago Bernabéu. Paolo Rossi had been banned for two years for match-fixing, returned to the squad, and then scored 6 goals including a hat-trick against Brazil.
facts.wowFactor: Paolo Rossi returned from a match-fixing ban to score 6 goals in the World Cup — including a hat-trick that eliminated Brazil.

facts.quizQuestion: Who won the first officially-awarded Golden Ball at the 1982 FIFA World Cup?
facts.explanation: The FIFA World Cup Golden Ball was officially introduced in 1982, and Paolo Rossi won it for his decisive contribution to Italy's third title. He also won the Golden Boot with 6 goals. Previous best-player awards in 1978 and 1974 were unofficial.
facts.wowFactor: The Golden Ball didn't officially exist before 1982 — and it debuted with one of the most dramatic redemption stories in sport.

facts.quizQuestion: Who won the 1982 World Cup Golden Boot with 6 goals, including a famous hat-trick against Brazil?
facts.explanation: Paolo Rossi's hat-trick against Brazil in the second round group stage — when Italy were considered underdogs — became one of the most celebrated performances in World Cup history. Brazil's brilliant team lost 3-2.
facts.wowFactor: Rossi scored a hat-trick against Brazil when nobody expected it — eliminating what many called the best team in the tournament.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1986 World Cup in Mexico, carried by the brilliance of Diego Maradona?
facts.explanation: Argentina defeated West Germany 3-2 in the final at the Estadio Azteca. Maradona won the Golden Ball after a tournament that featured arguably the two most famous goals in football history — in the same quarter-final match against England.
facts.wowFactor: Maradona carried Argentina to the 1986 title with one hand (literally) and one magical foot.

facts.quizQuestion: Who won the 1986 World Cup Golden Ball, the same tournament he scored both the 'Hand of God' and the 'Goal of the Century'?
facts.explanation: Diego Maradona's 1986 tournament is the greatest individual performance in World Cup history. In a single quarter-final against England, he scored an illegal handball goal and then a solo goal from his own half that beats five defenders.
facts.wowFactor: In one match against England, Maradona committed the greatest cheat and produced the greatest goal in World Cup history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which English striker won the 1986 Golden Boot with 6 goals, despite England losing to Maradona's Argentina?
facts.explanation: Gary Lineker scored 6 goals including a hat-trick against Poland to win the Golden Boot. He scored in the quarter-final against Argentina but England still lost 2-1 due to Maradona's two goals.
facts.wowFactor: Lineker won the Golden Boot — but two Maradona goals knocked him out. He couldn't be angry. Nobody could.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won the 1990 World Cup in Italy with a 1-0 final decided by a penalty — the lowest-scoring final in history?
facts.explanation: West Germany defeated Argentina 1-0 in the final in Rome with a late Andreas Brehme penalty. It was the last tournament West Germany competed in under that name — the following year, German reunification created a unified national team.
facts.wowFactor: West Germany's last ever final — Germany unified the next year and the name 'West Germany' vanished from football.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Italian striker won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot at the 1990 World Cup with 6 goals on home soil?
facts.explanation: Salvatore Schillaci ('Toto') was the unexpected hero of Italia '90, finishing as top scorer with 6 goals. He was a late inclusion to the squad and had barely featured for Italy before the tournament.
facts.wowFactor: Schillaci was almost left out of the squad. He ended up as the tournament's star, his eyes blazing through every goal celebration.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 1990 World Cup Final between West Germany and Argentina — the lowest-scoring final ever?
facts.explanation: West Germany beat Argentina 1-0 through an Andreas Brehme penalty. The match is remembered as one of the worst World Cup finals ever played — negative tactics dominated, and two Argentines were sent off.
facts.wowFactor: The 1990 final is widely considered the worst in history — 1-0 via a penalty, two red cards, no real football.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1994 World Cup in the United States — the first final decided on penalties?
facts.explanation: Brazil won on penalties after a 0-0 draw with Italy. Roberto Baggio, Italy's star player, missed the decisive penalty. It was Brazil's fourth World Cup title and Romário's crowning tournament.
facts.wowFactor: The first final decided by penalties — and Baggio's miss became one of sport's defining images of heartbreak.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the score (including penalty shootout) of the 1994 World Cup Final between Brazil and Italy?
facts.explanation: After 120 minutes of goalless football, Brazil beat Italy 3-2 on penalties. Roberto Baggio — the Golden Ball winner — took Italy's final penalty and blazed it over the bar, handing Brazil the trophy.
facts.wowFactor: Baggio: his ponytail, his genius, his tears. The man who defined a tournament — and the one penalty that undid it all.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian striker won the 1994 Golden Ball, forming a lethal partnership with Bebeto to win Brazil's fourth title?
facts.explanation: Romário and Bebeto's partnership at USA '94 is one of football's greatest. Their 'baby-rocking' goal celebration after Bebeto's goal against the Netherlands became iconic. Romário scored 5 goals and won the Golden Ball.
facts.wowFactor: Romário and Bebeto's baby-rocking goal celebration became one of football's most beloved moments — Bebeto's son had just been born.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1998 World Cup on home soil, with Zinedine Zidane scoring twice in the final?
facts.explanation: France defeated Brazil 3-0 in Paris, with Zidane scoring two headers in the first half. Brazil's star Ronaldo (Brazil) played despite a mysterious health episode the night before the match.
facts.wowFactor: Ronaldo (Brazil) played the 1998 final after a suspected seizure the night before — France destroyed them 3-0 with Zidane headers.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 1998 World Cup Final between France and Brazil in Paris?
facts.explanation: France won 3-0 — Zidane scored twice and Emmanuel Petit added a third late on. It remains one of the most dominant World Cup final victories and France's first-ever title.
facts.wowFactor: Zidane: two headers in the final. France 3-0 Brazil. The world barely believed what it was watching.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player won the 1998 Golden Ball despite Brazil losing the final 3-0 — a remarkable award for a tournament-long performance?
facts.explanation: Ronaldo (Brazil) won the Golden Ball for his exceptional play throughout the 1998 tournament, despite Brazil's defeat in the final. His mysterious illness the night before the final remains one of football's great unsolved mysteries.
facts.wowFactor: Ronaldo (Brazil) won the Golden Ball — for the tournament he played, not the final he stumbled through.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Croatian striker scored 6 goals at the 1998 World Cup as Croatia made a remarkable debut, finishing third?
facts.explanation: Davor Šuker won the Golden Boot with 6 goals as Croatia appeared in the World Cup for the first time and finished third. His lofted chip over Fabian Barthez vs Denmark is considered one of the tournament's finest goals.
facts.wowFactor: Croatia's World Cup debut — and their striker wins the Golden Boot. A debut for the ages.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2002 World Cup — the first held in Asia — defeating Germany in the final?
facts.explanation: Brazil defeated Germany 2-0 in Yokohama, with Ronaldo (Brazil) scoring both goals. It was Ronaldo's redemption after collapsing before the 1998 final. South Korea also made history by reaching the semi-finals.
facts.wowFactor: Ronaldo (Brazil): from mystery collapse in 1998 to two goals in the 2002 final. The greatest comeback in World Cup history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which German goalkeeper became the only goalkeeper ever to win the World Cup Golden Ball, at the 2002 tournament?
facts.explanation: Oliver Kahn was awarded the Golden Ball despite Germany losing the 2002 final to Brazil. He made extraordinary saves throughout the tournament and is the only goalkeeper in history to win football's top individual award at a World Cup.
facts.wowFactor: A goalkeeper won the Golden Ball. Oliver Kahn was so dominant that FIFA had no choice — goalkeepers had never done this before.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player won the 2002 Golden Boot with 8 goals, scoring twice in the final to give Brazil their fifth title?
facts.explanation: Ronaldo (Brazil) ended the tournament with 8 goals and the Golden Boot. His two goals in the final at Yokohama were a fitting end to his redemption arc from 1998. He wore his famous double-arc hairstyle throughout.
facts.wowFactor: The alien haircut was the talk of 2002 — Ronaldo (Brazil) claimed he did it so his son could pick him out on TV.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2006 World Cup in Germany, after Zinedine Zidane was sent off in the final for a headbutt?
facts.explanation: Italy defeated France on penalties after a 1-1 draw. Zidane headbutted Marco Materazzi in extra time and was sent off in his final professional match — yet still won the Golden Ball.
facts.wowFactor: Zidane played the last game of his career, got sent off for a headbutt, and still won the tournament's best player award.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score (including penalty shootout) of the 2006 World Cup Final between Italy and France?
facts.explanation: Italy beat France on penalties after a 1-1 draw. Zidane opened the scoring with a chipped Panenka penalty, then was sent off for his headbutt on Materazzi before the shootout. Italy's Fabio Grosso scored the winning penalty.
facts.wowFactor: Zidane scored a Panenka chip, got a red card for headbutting someone, and France still nearly won on penalties.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player won the 2006 Golden Ball despite receiving a red card for a headbutt in the final?
facts.explanation: Zinedine Zidane won the Golden Ball for the 2006 World Cup even though he was sent off in the final for headbutting Marco Materazzi. The award was based on his performances throughout the tournament. He retired immediately after.
facts.wowFactor: Voted best player of the tournament. Red-carded in the final. Retired forever 20 minutes later. Peak Zidane.

facts.quizQuestion: Which German striker won the 2006 Golden Boot with 5 goals, continuing his path toward the all-time World Cup scoring record?
facts.explanation: Miroslav Klose scored 5 goals for hosts Germany in 2006. Combined with his 5 in 2002, he was building toward the all-time record he would eventually claim in 2014 with 16 career World Cup goals.
facts.wowFactor: Klose scored at four World Cups. Every single tournament, he scored. In 2014 he finally broke the all-time record.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2010 World Cup in South Africa — the first World Cup held on African soil?
facts.explanation: Spain defeated the Netherlands 1-0 after extra time with Andrés Iniesta's goal in the 116th minute. It was Spain's first World Cup title, part of a golden era in which they also won Euro 2008 and Euro 2012.
facts.wowFactor: Spain won the 2010 World Cup as part of a run of three consecutive major tournament victories — the most dominant era in international football history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Uruguayan striker won the 2010 Golden Ball, leading Uruguay to a surprise fourth-place finish?
facts.explanation: Diego Forlán won the Golden Ball at the 2010 World Cup in South Africa, excelling for Uruguay throughout. He scored stunning long-range goals and led Uruguay — a small nation — to the semi-finals for the first time since 1970.
facts.wowFactor: Forlán won the best player award at a tournament his country nearly won — and he did it with goals that defied physics.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2014 World Cup in Brazil, including a 7-1 semi-final demolition of the host nation?
facts.explanation: Germany defeated Argentina 1-0 in extra time in the final. Earlier they had destroyed hosts Brazil 7-1 in the semi-final — the most shocking result in World Cup history — scoring 5 goals in 18 minutes.
facts.wowFactor: Germany beat Brazil 7-1 on Brazilian soil. They had to win four more games to get the trophy they'd just earned in 18 minutes.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player won the 2014 Golden Ball despite Argentina losing the final — his first of an eventual two Golden Balls?
facts.explanation: Lionel Messi won the 2014 Golden Ball for his performances throughout the tournament. Despite this, many felt Messi was not at his best in the final and that the award was controversial. He later won the 2022 Golden Ball in far more deserved circumstances.
facts.wowFactor: Messi won the 2014 Golden Ball and many thought he didn't deserve it. He won the 2022 Golden Ball and nobody doubted it.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Colombian midfielder won the 2014 Golden Boot with 6 goals, launching a spectacular international career?
facts.explanation: James Rodríguez was the revelation of the 2014 World Cup in Brazil. His six goals included a stunning chest-and-volley against Uruguay that is considered one of the greatest goals in World Cup history.
facts.wowFactor: James Rodríguez's volley against Uruguay — chest control, turn, thundering left-foot strike. An instant World Cup classic.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2018 World Cup in Russia, defeating Croatia 4-2 in the final?
facts.explanation: France defeated Croatia 4-2 in Moscow, with Kylian Mbappé becoming the second teenager to score in a World Cup final (after Pelé in 1958). An own goal from Mario Mandžukić opened the scoring — bizarrely, Mandžukić also scored for Croatia.
facts.wowFactor: Mandžukić scored an own goal and a regular goal in the same final — the only player to do so in World Cup final history.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 2018 World Cup Final between France and Croatia in Moscow?
facts.explanation: France beat Croatia 4-2 in a high-quality final at the Luzhniki Stadium. The match featured VAR controversy (a soft handball penalty for France), two own goals, and Mbappé's thunderous strike.
facts.wowFactor: The 2018 final had everything: VAR drama, own goals, teenager heroics. The scoreline tells half the story.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Croatian player won the 2018 Golden Ball, ending Messi and Ronaldo's decade-long domination of major individual awards?
facts.explanation: Luka Modrić's 2018 Golden Ball was the first awarded to someone other than Messi or Ronaldo in a World Cup since 2006. He led Croatia — a nation of 4 million — to their first-ever World Cup final.
facts.wowFactor: Luka Modrić broke Messi and Ronaldo's decade-long grip on football's top awards — and his country had 4 million people.

facts.quizQuestion: Which English striker won the 2018 Golden Boot with 6 goals, including several penalties?
facts.explanation: Harry Kane scored 6 goals to win the Golden Boot, though three of his six goals were penalties. England reached the semi-finals for the first time since 1990, losing to Croatia in extra time.
facts.wowFactor: Kane's 6 goals gave England their best World Cup run in 28 years — though Croatia ended the dream in extra time.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2022 World Cup in Qatar — in what is widely regarded as the greatest final ever played?
facts.explanation: Argentina defeated France on penalties after a 3-3 draw. Mbappé scored a hat-trick to drag France back from 2-0 down, forcing extra time. Messi won the Golden Ball — his second — to complete his World Cup legacy.
facts.wowFactor: Argentina 3-3 France — Mbappé's hat-trick, Messi's tears, penalties. The greatest final in 92 years of World Cups.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the score (including penalty shootout) of the 2022 World Cup Final between Argentina and France?
facts.explanation: Argentina led 2-0 with 10 minutes left. Mbappé scored twice in 97 seconds to level, then both sides scored in extra time to make it 3-3. Argentina won 4-2 on penalties. Mbappé ended with 8 goals — the Golden Boot.
facts.wowFactor: 2-0 up with 10 minutes left, then 3-3. Then penalties. The 2022 final redefined what a World Cup final could be.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player won the 2022 Golden Ball — his second — cementing his status as the greatest player in World Cup history?
facts.explanation: Lionel Messi's 2022 Golden Ball, alongside his winner's medal, finally silenced those who questioned his World Cup legacy. He scored 7 goals and provided 3 assists across the tournament, including a goal in the final.
facts.wowFactor: The greatest player ever, at his final World Cup, wins it at last. Messi in 2022 is the most complete World Cup performance ever.

facts.quizQuestion: Which French striker won the 2022 Golden Boot with 8 goals, including a hat-trick in the final?
facts.explanation: Kylian Mbappé scored 8 goals to win the Golden Boot at Qatar 2022, including three in the final alone. Despite this extraordinary performance, France lost on penalties to Argentina. He was 23 years old.
facts.wowFactor: Mbappé scored a hat-trick in the World Cup final — and still ended up on the losing side. He was 23.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the inaugural FIFA Women's World Cup in 1991, held in China?
facts.explanation: The United States defeated Norway 2-1 in the final at Tianhe Stadium, Guangzhou. The tournament was officially called the '1st FIFA World Championship for Women's Football' and played in 80-minute matches.
facts.wowFactor: The first Women's World Cup in history — and the US have never let anyone forget who won it.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country hosted the inaugural FIFA Women's World Cup in 1991?
facts.explanation: China hosted the first Women's World Cup in November 1991. The tournament was seen as an experiment by FIFA and featured 12 teams. Despite expectations, the US beat the more established Norwegian team in the final.
facts.wowFactor: China hosted the birth of the Women's World Cup — and their women's team nearly won it in 1999 on the other side of the world.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American player won the Golden Ball at the inaugural 1991 Women's World Cup?
facts.explanation: Carin Jennings won the Golden Ball at the 1991 Women's World Cup for her outstanding performances. Her partner Michelle Akers won the Golden Boot with 10 goals, creating a formidable American duo in the inaugural tournament.
facts.wowFactor: Two American players dominated the first Women's World Cup — setting the tone for three decades of US dominance.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American striker scored 10 goals at the inaugural 1991 Women's World Cup, including two in the final?
facts.explanation: Michelle Akers scored 10 goals in the 1991 Women's World Cup — including two in the final against Norway. She is considered one of the pioneers of women's football globally and helped establish the US as the dominant force.
facts.wowFactor: 10 goals in a single Women's World Cup — a record that stood for decades and announced the USA's arrival as a global force.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1995 FIFA Women's World Cup in Sweden, claiming their only World Cup title?
facts.explanation: Norway defeated Germany 2-0 in the final at Råsunda Stadium, Stockholm. Led by Hege Riise who won the Golden Ball, it remains Norway's only Women's World Cup championship.
facts.wowFactor: Norway's one and only Women's World Cup title — and they did it in style, beating Germany in the final.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Scandinavian country hosted the 1995 FIFA Women's World Cup?
facts.explanation: Sweden hosted the 1995 Women's World Cup. Norway defeated hosts' neighbor in the final. The tournament saw 8 teams compete, with the format expanded for subsequent editions.
facts.wowFactor: Sweden hosted — and their Nordic neighbors Norway won. Scandinavia's finest hour in women's football.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Norwegian midfielder won the Golden Ball at the 1995 Women's World Cup?
facts.explanation: Hege Riise was the dominant player of the 1995 Women's World Cup, helping Norway to their only title. She was a creative midfielder who also scored important goals throughout the tournament.
facts.wowFactor: Hege Riise: Norway's greatest women's footballer, Golden Ball winner, World Cup champion — in the same summer.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 1999 Women's World Cup in front of 90,185 fans at the Rose Bowl — a record crowd for women's sports?
facts.explanation: The United States defeated China on penalties at the Rose Bowl, Pasadena. Brandi Chastain's winning penalty and her famous jersey-removal celebration became one of the most iconic images in sports history.
facts.wowFactor: 90,185 people packed the Rose Bowl — the largest crowd in women's sports history. Then Chastain scored and took her shirt off.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the score (including penalty shootout) of the 1999 Women's World Cup Final between the United States and China?
facts.explanation: After 120 goalless minutes, the United States beat China 5-4 on penalties. Brandi Chastain — who had previously scored an own goal — stepped up to score the winning penalty. She removed her jersey in celebration.
facts.wowFactor: The same player who scored the own goal then scored the winning penalty. Sport doesn't write scripts like this.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Chinese player won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot at the 1999 Women's World Cup, despite China losing in the final?
facts.explanation: Sun Wen won both the Golden Ball and shared the Golden Boot (7 goals, tied with Brazil's Sissi) at the 1999 Women's World Cup. She was the driving force behind China's run to the final and is considered China's greatest female footballer.
facts.wowFactor: Sun Wen was so dominant that she won the tournament's top individual awards — while her team lost on penalties.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2003 Women's World Cup — a tournament moved from China to the United States due to SARS?
facts.explanation: Germany defeated Sweden 2-1 in golden goal extra time to claim their first Women's World Cup. The tournament was moved from China to the US at the last minute due to the SARS outbreak. Nia Künzer scored the golden goal.
facts.wowFactor: Germany's first Women's World Cup — won with a golden goal, in a tournament that almost didn't happen due to SARS.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country stepped in to host the 2003 Women's World Cup after the original host was affected by a SARS outbreak?
facts.explanation: China was originally awarded the 2003 Women's World Cup but the SARS epidemic forced a last-minute transfer of hosting to the United States. The US had successfully hosted in 1999, so the infrastructure was already in place.
facts.wowFactor: SARS changed history — the US became the only nation to host the Women's World Cup twice, both times as emergency or planned host.

facts.quizQuestion: Which German striker won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot at the 2003 Women's World Cup?
facts.explanation: Birgit Prinz scored 7 goals and won both awards as Germany won their first Women's World Cup in the United States. Prinz was the best women's player in the world during the 2003-2007 period.
facts.wowFactor: Birgit Prinz: top scorer, best player, World Cup winner. Germany's era of women's football dominance had begun.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2007 Women's World Cup in China, retaining the title with a 2-0 final over Brazil?
facts.explanation: Germany defeated Brazil 2-0 in the Shanghai final to retain the Women's World Cup. They did not concede a single goal throughout the tournament — scoring 21 goals in 6 games. Brazil's Marta won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot.
facts.wowFactor: Germany won the 2007 Women's World Cup without conceding a single goal — 6 games, 21 scored, 0 against.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian player won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot at the 2007 Women's World Cup, despite Brazil losing the final?
facts.explanation: Marta won the Golden Ball and Golden Boot with 7 goals at the 2007 Women's World Cup in China. Despite this, Germany won the tournament. Marta — known as 'Pelé with skirts' in Brazil — was the world's best female player of her era.
facts.wowFactor: Marta won the best player award while her team lost. In women's football, she is what Pelé was to the men's game.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won the 2011 Women's World Cup months after the devastating Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami?
facts.explanation: Japan defeated the United States on penalties in the final in Frankfurt, after Homare Sawa's 117th-minute equalizer forced extra time. Japan had qualified for the tournament just four months after the March 2011 earthquake that killed nearly 16,000 people.
facts.wowFactor: Japan won the Women's World Cup four months after a devastating earthquake killed 16,000 of their countrymen. Sport rarely means more.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the score (including penalty shootout) of the 2011 Women's World Cup Final between Japan and the United States?
facts.explanation: Japan and the United States drew 2-2 after extra time, with Homare Sawa's equalizer in the 117th minute. Japan won the shootout 3-1 in a result that sent the Japanese nation into euphoria after a year of tragedy.
facts.wowFactor: 117th minute. The goalkeeper had already started cheering. Then Homare Sawa tapped it in. Japan went mad.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Japanese player won both the 2011 Golden Ball and Golden Boot, scoring a 117th-minute equalizer in the final?
facts.explanation: Homare Sawa's late equalizer in the 2011 final is one of the greatest moments in Women's World Cup history. She scored 5 goals and won both awards. She retired after playing in six World Cups across her career.
facts.wowFactor: 117th minute. Losing. Tournament effectively over. Homare Sawa tapped in the equalizer and Japan went on to win it all.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2015 Women's World Cup in Canada with a record 5-2 final victory over Japan?
facts.explanation: The United States demolished Japan 5-2 in the final at BC Place Stadium, Vancouver. Carli Lloyd scored a hat-trick in the first 16 minutes, including a remarkable goal from the halfway line.
facts.wowFactor: 16 minutes in: 3-0. Carli Lloyd scored from the halfway line. The 2015 final was already over before halftime.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score when the United States beat Japan in the 2015 Women's World Cup Final — the highest-scoring women's final ever?
facts.explanation: USA won 5-2, the most goals ever scored in a Women's World Cup Final. Carli Lloyd scored 3 times in the opening 16 minutes, with her most famous goal — a lob from near the halfway line — making it 4-0.
facts.wowFactor: USA scored 4 goals in the first 16 minutes against Japan. The second half was technically a formality.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American player won the 2015 Golden Ball after scoring a hat-trick in the first 16 minutes of the final?
facts.explanation: Carli Lloyd's performance in the 2015 final is the greatest individual display in Women's World Cup Final history. Her hat-trick — including a lob from the halfway line — came in the opening 16 minutes as the USA won 5-2.
facts.wowFactor: Hat-trick in 16 minutes. Goal from the halfway line. Golden Ball winner. Carli Lloyd in 2015 is the Women's WC Final at its peak.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2019 Women's World Cup in France, their fourth title — the most of any nation?
facts.explanation: The United States defeated the Netherlands 2-0 in Lyon, claiming a record fourth Women's World Cup title. The US team became famous for their equal pay advocacy as much as their play. Megan Rapinoe won both Golden Ball and Boot.
facts.wowFactor: Four Women's World Cups. No other nation has come close to what the United States have built in women's football.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American player won both the 2019 Golden Ball and Golden Boot while also becoming known for her equal pay activism?
facts.explanation: Megan Rapinoe won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot at France 2019, scoring 6 goals. She became one of the tournament's most visible figures due to her outspoken political positions as much as her football.
facts.wowFactor: Rapinoe's 2019: Golden Ball, Golden Boot, and a political movement. Football's most complete package that year.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country won the 2023 Women's World Cup in Australia and New Zealand — their first-ever Women's World Cup title?
facts.explanation: Spain defeated England 1-0 in the final at Stadium Australia, Sydney, before 75,784 fans. It was Spain's first Women's World Cup, capping a golden era for La Roja Femenina. Aitana Bonmatí won the Golden Ball.
facts.wowFactor: Spain arrived at the 2023 Women's World Cup after a team revolt against the coach — and won the whole thing anyway.

facts.quizQuestion: Which co-hosting nations staged the 2023 FIFA Women's World Cup — the first expanded 32-team tournament?
facts.explanation: Australia and New Zealand co-hosted the 2023 Women's World Cup — the first to feature 32 teams and the first Women's World Cup in the southern hemisphere. Spain won the tournament; Australia reached the semi-finals.
facts.wowFactor: The Women's World Cup expanded to 32 teams in 2023 — the same year Australia reached the semi-finals on home soil.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spanish midfielder won the 2023 Golden Ball and later won the Ballon d'Or Féminin in the same calendar year?
facts.explanation: Aitana Bonmatí's 2023 was the most dominant year by any women's footballer: she won the Women's World Cup, the Golden Ball, and the Ballon d'Or Féminin. She was 25 years old — her career was just beginning.
facts.wowFactor: Women's World Cup Golden Ball + Ballon d'Or in the same year. Aitana Bonmatí at 25 — potentially the greatest yet to come.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Japanese player won the 2023 Women's World Cup Golden Boot with 5 goals, becoming a breakout star?
facts.explanation: Hinata Miyazawa of Japan scored 5 goals to win the Golden Boot at the 2023 Women's World Cup. Japan were knocked out in the round of 16, but Miyazawa's performances announced her as one of the sport's emerging stars.
facts.wowFactor: Miyazawa won the Golden Boot in a tournament Japan were eliminated in the round of 16 — individual brilliance in collective disappointment.

facts.quizQuestion: At which iconic Californian stadium was the 1999 FIFA Women's World Cup Final held — setting a women's-sports attendance record of 90,185 that still stands today?
facts.explanation: The 1999 Women's World Cup Final was held at the Rose Bowl in Pasadena, California, before a record 90,185 fans — still the largest crowd at any Women's World Cup Final. The Rose Bowl also hosted the 1994 Men's World Cup Final.
facts.wowFactor: 90,185 fans at the Rose Bowl in 1999. The record still stands. The Women's World Cup has never filled a stadium like that since.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Pelé make his FIFA World Cup debut, at the age of 17?
facts.explanation: Pelé debuted at the 1958 World Cup in Sweden, becoming the youngest player to score in a World Cup final. He scored 6 goals including two in the final as Brazil defeated Sweden 5-2.
facts.wowFactor: 17 years old. His first World Cup. Six goals. Youngest ever scorer in a World Cup final. An arrival the world has never forgotten.

facts.quizQuestion: How many FIFA World Cup titles did Pelé win during his career — a record no other player has matched?
facts.explanation: Pelé won three World Cups: 1958 (at 17), 1962, and 1970. He is the only player in history to have won three FIFA World Cup titles. His 1962 tournament was interrupted by injury, and Garrincha carried Brazil in his absence.
facts.wowFactor: Three World Cups. The only player in history to do it. Pelé is not just Brazil's greatest — he is the sport's greatest achievement.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Pelé score across his World Cup career (1958, 1962, 1966, 1970)?
facts.explanation: Pelé scored 12 goals across four World Cups. He scored 6 in 1958 (including 2 in the final), 1 in 1962 before injury ended his tournament, 1 in 1966, and 4 in 1970. Only Klose (16), Ronaldo Brazil (15), and Müller (14) scored more.
facts.wowFactor: 12 World Cup goals — and he played through an injury in 1966 when defenders were literally trying to kick him out of the tournament.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Diego Maradona win the FIFA World Cup Golden Ball, in the tournament where he scored both the 'Hand of God' and 'Goal of the Century'?
facts.explanation: Maradona's 1986 World Cup is the greatest individual tournament performance ever recorded. In the quarter-final against England, he scored an illegal handball goal and then a solo masterpiece. Argentina went on to win the title.
facts.wowFactor: 1986: the year one man carried an entire World Cup on his shoulders — and scored football's two most famous goals in the same match.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Diego Maradona represent at the FIFA World Cup?
facts.explanation: Maradona represented Argentina in four World Cups (1982, 1986, 1990, 1994). He won the title in 1986 and reached the final in 1990. He was born in Lanús, Buenos Aires Province, Argentina on October 30, 1960.
facts.wowFactor: A boy from a Buenos Aires shantytown became the savior of an entire nation's football identity.

facts.quizQuestion: How many FIFA World Cup Golden Ball awards has Lionel Messi won — a record no other player has achieved?
facts.explanation: Messi won the Golden Ball in 2014 (controversially, as Argentina lost the final) and again in 2022 (universally deserved, as he scored 7 goals and won the title). He is the only player to win the award twice.
facts.wowFactor: Two Golden Balls. Nobody else has even won it twice. Messi is in a category of one.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Miroslav Klose score across his World Cup career — the all-time record?
facts.explanation: Miroslav Klose scored 16 World Cup goals across four tournaments (2002, 2006, 2010, 2014), breaking Ronaldo (Brazil)'s record of 15 at the 2014 World Cup in Brazil. He scored his record-breaker against Brazil, in Brazil.
facts.wowFactor: Klose broke the all-time scoring record in Brazil — against Brazil. The poetry was not lost on anyone.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Miroslav Klose, the all-time World Cup top scorer, represent at the FIFA World Cup?
facts.explanation: Miroslav Klose was born in Opole, Poland (then Communist Poland) to a German family, and chose to represent Germany internationally. He played from 2002 to 2014, scoring 16 goals across four World Cups.
facts.wowFactor: Born in Poland, played for Germany — and scored more World Cup goals than anyone in history.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Just Fontaine score at the 1958 World Cup — the all-time record for a single tournament?
facts.explanation: Just Fontaine scored 13 goals in 6 games at the 1958 World Cup in Sweden. He averaged more than 2 goals per match. The record has now stood for 65+ years and is widely considered one of sport's most unbreakable records.
facts.wowFactor: 13 goals in 6 games. The record has stood for 65 years. Just Fontaine played his only World Cup and left the most extraordinary individual mark ever.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Johan Cruyff represent at the 1974 World Cup, where he invented the famous Cruyff Turn?
facts.explanation: Johan Cruyff led the Netherlands to the 1974 World Cup Final with his revolutionary 'Total Football'. He performed the iconic Cruyff Turn against Swedish defender Jan Olsson in the group stage. Netherlands lost the final to West Germany.
facts.wowFactor: Cruyff turned Jan Olsson inside out with one movement — and in doing so, he invented a skill practiced by millions of children every day.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Zinedine Zidane win the World Cup Golden Ball despite being sent off for a headbutt in the final?
facts.explanation: Zidane won the 2006 Golden Ball in his final professional match — a match he was sent off in for headbutting Marco Materazzi. France lost on penalties to Italy. He scored a Panenka penalty earlier in the match.
facts.wowFactor: Zidane won the tournament's best player award in the same match where he got a red card and immediately retired.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Ronaldo (Brazil) score across his World Cup career — the second-highest total in history?
facts.explanation: Ronaldo (Brazil) scored 15 World Cup goals across four tournaments (1994, 1998, 2002, 2006). He held the all-time record until Klose broke it in 2014. His 2002 tournament (8 goals) was the greatest redemption story in World Cup history.
facts.wowFactor: 15 World Cup goals — the record for 16 years, until a German came along in Brazil to beat it.

facts.quizQuestion: How many FIFA World Cup titles did Ronaldo (Brazil) win during his career?
facts.explanation: Ronaldo (Brazil) won two World Cups — 1994 (as a substitute who barely played) and 2002 (as the undisputed star, scoring 8 goals including 2 in the final). The 2002 title was his redemption after his mysterious collapse before the 1998 final.
facts.wowFactor: 1994: barely played. 1998: collapsed the night before the final. 2002: scored twice in the final. The greatest comeback in World Cup history.

facts.quizQuestion: How old was Kylian Mbappé when he won the 2018 World Cup with France — the second teenager to win the World Cup?
facts.explanation: Mbappé won the 2018 World Cup at age 19, becoming only the second teenager to win the tournament (Pelé was the first in 1958). He scored 4 goals including one in the final and was named the best young player of the tournament.
facts.wowFactor: The second teenager to win the World Cup — Pelé in 1958, Mbappé in 2018. Sixty years apart, same unstoppable quality.

facts.quizQuestion: How many World Cup goals did Gerd Müller score across his career — the third-highest total in history?
facts.explanation: Gerd Müller scored 14 World Cup goals across two tournaments: 10 in 1970 and 4 in 1974. He held the all-time record until Ronaldo (Brazil) broke it in 2006. Known as 'Der Bomber', he was the most lethal penalty box striker of his era.
facts.wowFactor: 14 World Cup goals in just two tournaments — Müller's scoring rate was higher than both Klose and Ronaldo (Brazil) who later beat his record.

facts.quizQuestion: Franz Beckenbauer is one of only two people to win the World Cup as both captain and manager. Which nation did he lead to both victories?
facts.explanation: Franz Beckenbauer captained West Germany to the 1974 World Cup title on home soil, then managed West Germany to the 1990 World Cup title in Italy. Only Didier Deschamps has matched this feat (as France captain in 1998, manager in 2018).
facts.wowFactor: Beckenbauer lifted the World Cup as captain in 1974, then watched it as manager in 1990. Sixteen years — same result.

facts.quizQuestion: Paolo Rossi had been banned for two years for match-fixing before returning to win the World Cup with which country in 1982?
facts.explanation: Rossi served a two-year ban for alleged match-fixing, returned to the Italian squad for the 1982 World Cup, then went on to score 6 goals — including a hat-trick against Brazil — and win both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot.
facts.wowFactor: Banned for two years, barely match fit — then hat-tricks, Golden Ball, Golden Boot, World Cup. Sport's greatest redemption arc.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Luka Modrić win the FIFA World Cup Golden Ball, ending Messi and Ronaldo's decade-long grip on major awards?
facts.explanation: Luka Modrić's 2018 Golden Ball was the first non-Messi/non-Ronaldo Golden Ball since Zidane in 2006. He led Croatia — a nation of 4 million — to the final where they lost 4-2 to France.
facts.wowFactor: A player from a 4-million-person country broke the most famous duopoly in sport's history. Modrić in 2018 was unignorable.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Eusébio score at the 1966 World Cup in England — the most by any player in that tournament?
facts.explanation: Eusébio scored 9 goals in the 1966 World Cup, winning the Golden Boot by a wide margin. His most extraordinary performance came in the quarter-final against North Korea — Portugal were 3-0 down at half time, and Eusébio scored 4 goals to win 5-3.
facts.wowFactor: Portugal 0-3 North Korea at half time. Eusébio then scored 4 goals in one half. Portugal won 5-3. Sport's greatest comeback.

facts.quizQuestion: Garrincha — the winger who carried his team to the 1962 World Cup title after Pelé's injury — represented which country?
facts.explanation: Garrincha (Manuel Francisco dos Santos) was a Brazilian winger regarded as the greatest dribbler of all time. He was born with deformities in both legs yet became the most feared winger in world football. He won back-to-back World Cups with Brazil in 1958 and 1962.
facts.wowFactor: Garrincha was born with both legs bent inward. He became the greatest dribbler football has ever seen.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals has Marta scored in the Women's World Cup across her career — the all-time record as of 2023?
facts.explanation: Marta scored 17 Women's World Cup goals across six tournaments (2003-2023) for Brazil. She is the all-time top scorer and has been named FIFA World Player of the Year six times. She holds the record the way Klose holds the men's record.
facts.wowFactor: 17 Women's World Cup goals. As of 2023, Marta holds the record for the most by any player — man or woman — in their respective Women's World Cup.

facts.quizQuestion: In how many FIFA Women's World Cups has Marta competed for Brazil?
facts.explanation: Marta played in six Women's World Cups for Brazil (2003, 2007, 2011, 2015, 2019, 2023), scoring 17 goals across all of them. Despite her brilliance, Brazil never won the Women's World Cup during her career.
facts.wowFactor: Six World Cups. 17 goals. Zero trophies. Marta carried Brazil to five finals but never won. The game's greatest unfulfilled genius.

facts.quizQuestion: How many international goals did Abby Wambach score during her career — a world record at the time of her retirement?
facts.explanation: Abby Wambach scored 184 international goals for the United States, a world record at the time of her retirement in 2015. The record was later broken by Christine Sinclair. Wambach's most famous goal was a 122nd-minute header against Brazil in 2011.
facts.wowFactor: 184 international goals. A record that stood for years. Wambach scored more than any man or woman in football history — at that time.

facts.quizQuestion: How many Women's World Cup titles did Birgit Prinz win with Germany — both as Golden Ball winner (2003) and runner-up (2007)?
facts.explanation: Birgit Prinz won back-to-back Women's World Cups with Germany (2003 and 2007). She won the Golden Ball and Boot in 2003. Widely regarded as one of the greatest women's players of all time, she scored 14 World Cup goals in her career.
facts.wowFactor: Birgit Prinz in 2003: top scorer, best player, World Cup winner. In 2007: she did it again — minus the individual awards.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Sun Wen — winner of the 1999 Women's World Cup Golden Ball and Golden Boot — represent?
facts.explanation: Sun Wen is China's greatest female footballer. She won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot (7 goals, tied with Sissi of Brazil) at the 1999 Women's World Cup, despite China losing the final on penalties to the United States.
facts.wowFactor: China's greatest women's footballer won the tournament's top two awards in 1999 — while her team lost the final.

facts.quizQuestion: In how many minutes did Carli Lloyd score her hat-trick in the 2015 Women's World Cup Final — including a goal from the halfway line?
facts.explanation: Carli Lloyd scored three goals in the opening 16 minutes of the 2015 Women's World Cup Final against Japan. Her third goal — a lob from near the halfway line that sailed over the goalkeeper — is one of the greatest goals in World Cup Final history.
facts.wowFactor: Hat-trick in 16 minutes. The third goal from the halfway line. The 2015 final was effectively over before the first water break.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Homare Sawa make her Women's World Cup debut — beginning a career in which she played in six tournaments?
facts.explanation: Homare Sawa debuted at the 1995 Women's World Cup in Sweden and played in six consecutive tournaments through 2015. Her 117th-minute equalizer in the 2011 final remains the most dramatic goal in Women's World Cup history.
facts.wowFactor: Six Women's World Cups. The final chapter: a 117th-minute equalizer that won Japan the title. Homare Sawa played the full epic.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Sam Kerr score a stunning last-minute volley against England in a Women's World Cup semi-final on home soil?
facts.explanation: Sam Kerr came off the bench in the 2023 Women's World Cup semi-final and scored a breathtaking volley against England at Stadium Australia, Sydney. Australia eventually lost 3-1 but Kerr's goal — in front of a home crowd — was one of the tournament's defining moments.
facts.wowFactor: Coming off the bench against England, on home soil, in a semi-final — Sam Kerr's volley was the perfect Australian moment in women's football.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Aitana Bonmatí represent when she won both the 2023 Women's World Cup Golden Ball and the Ballon d'Or Féminin?
facts.explanation: Aitana Bonmatí is the creative heart of Spain's women's team. In 2023, she won the Women's World Cup with Spain, was awarded the Golden Ball, and then won the Ballon d'Or Féminin — one of the most complete individual seasons in football history.
facts.wowFactor: World Cup. Golden Ball. Ballon d'Or. All in 2023. At 25. Aitana Bonmatí is the beginning of something, not the peak.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did the 'Maracanazo' take place — Uruguay's shock 2-1 victory over Brazil in the World Cup's decisive final match?
facts.explanation: The Maracanazo occurred in 1950, when Uruguay defeated Brazil 2-1 before 173,850 fans at the Maracanã Stadium. Brazil only needed a draw to win the tournament. The silence that fell over the stadium is described as one of sport's most eerie moments.
facts.wowFactor: 200,000 Brazilians expected a coronation. Instead, Uruguay scored twice in the second half. The silence was heard around the world.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 'Maracanazo' — Uruguay's shock World Cup victory over Brazil in 1950?
facts.explanation: Uruguay defeated Brazil 2-1, with goals from Juan Schiaffino and Alcides Ghiggia overturning Friaça's early Brazilian opener. Ghiggia's winner was scored at the near post — a position Brazil's goalkeeper Moacir Barbosa had reportedly left uncovered.
facts.wowFactor: Ghiggia: 'Only three people have silenced the Maracanã — Frank Sinatra, the Pope, and me.'

facts.quizQuestion: Which country was hosting the 1954 World Cup when the 'Miracle of Bern' occurred — West Germany defeating Hungary in the final?
facts.explanation: Switzerland hosted the 1954 World Cup. The final in Bern saw West Germany defeat Hungary's 'Golden Team' 3-2, despite Hungary having beaten them 8-3 earlier in the tournament. The victory became synonymous with West Germany's post-war national identity.
facts.wowFactor: West Germany in 1954 didn't just win a football match — they won back their dignity in a country they'd helped destroy nine years earlier.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the 'Miracle of Bern' — the 1954 World Cup Final between West Germany and Hungary?
facts.explanation: West Germany won 3-2 against Hungary's supposedly unbeatable 'Golden Team'. Hungary led 2-0 early in the match. Helmut Rahn scored twice, including the winner 6 minutes from time. The match is called the greatest sporting upset of the 20th century in Germany.
facts.wowFactor: Hungary led 2-0. Hungary were unbeaten in 4 years. Hungary lost. The Miracle of Bern is Germany's sporting foundation myth.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player scored both the 'Hand of God' and the 'Goal of the Century' in the same 1986 World Cup quarter-final against England?
facts.explanation: Diego Maradona scored both goals in Argentina's 2-1 victory over England. The first was an illegal handball he later described as scored 'a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God.' The second was a 65-metre solo run past five defenders.
facts.wowFactor: The greatest cheat and the greatest goal — both by the same player, in the same match, four minutes apart. Nobody else could have done either.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Maradona score the 'Hand of God' goal against England in the World Cup quarter-final?
facts.explanation: The Hand of God goal was scored on June 22, 1986, at the Estadio Azteca, Mexico City. Maradona punched the ball into the net, the referee missed it, and Argentina won 2-1. Maradona later admitted the handball in a 2005 autobiography.
facts.wowFactor: Maradona lied about it for 20 years. Then he told the truth — and said it was one of his happiest moments.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Johan Cruyff perform his iconic 'Cruyff Turn' — now practiced by footballers worldwide — at the FIFA World Cup?
facts.explanation: Cruyff performed the turn against Swedish defender Jan Olsson in the 1974 group stage. He feigned to cross the ball, dragged it behind his standing leg, and turned Olsson in a single movement. The skill has been practiced by millions of players ever since.
facts.wowFactor: Jan Olsson reacted to Cruyff's dummy and kicked air. He described the moment: 'My world fell apart.' A move was born.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Roberto Baggio's missed penalty send Brazil the World Cup in the first final decided by a shootout?
facts.explanation: In 1994 at the Rose Bowl, Pasadena, Roberto Baggio stepped up to take Italy's fifth penalty and blazed it over the crossbar. Brazil won the shootout 3-2 and their fourth World Cup title. Baggio stood still, hands on knees, as Brazil celebrated.
facts.wowFactor: Baggio had dragged Italy to the final almost single-handedly. Then he put the deciding penalty in Row Z. Sport is cruel.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player headbutted Marco Materazzi in the 2006 World Cup Final — in the last match of his career — and received a red card?
facts.explanation: Zinedine Zidane headbutted Italian defender Marco Materazzi in extra time of the 2006 final after a verbal altercation. He was sent off and France went on to lose on penalties. He had opened the scoring with a Panenka chip — and still won the Golden Ball.
facts.wowFactor: Last professional match. Red card for a headbutt. Golden Ball winner. France lost. Zidane could not do things the ordinary way.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of Germany's 2014 World Cup semi-final demolition of host nation Brazil — the most shocking result in World Cup history?
facts.explanation: Germany defeated Brazil 7-1 at the Estádio Mineirão in Belo Horizonte, scoring 5 goals in an 18-minute second-half spell. The 'Mineirazo' left Brazil's players and fans in tears of shock. Germany went on to win the final 1-0 against Argentina.
facts.wowFactor: 5 goals in 18 minutes. Brazil fans crying in the stands. The 7-1 is still the most unbelievable score in World Cup history.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Germany defeat host nation Brazil 7-1 in the World Cup semi-final — the event known as the 'Mineirazo'?
facts.explanation: The Mineirazo occurred on July 8, 2014 at the Estádio Mineirão. Germany's five-goal blitz in 18 minutes of the second half left Brazil's players shattered. Germany won the tournament four days later.
facts.wowFactor: July 8, 2014. The day Brazil lost 7-1 at home. Every Brazilian alive remembers where they were.

facts.quizQuestion: Which African nation became the first from the African continent and the Arab world to reach a FIFA World Cup semi-final, at the 2022 tournament in Qatar?
facts.explanation: Morocco defeated Spain (on penalties) and Portugal to reach the 2022 semi-finals — the first African team and first Arab nation to achieve this. They finished fourth, defeating Croatia 2-1 in the third-place match.
facts.wowFactor: Morocco defeated Spain and Portugal to reach the semi-final. Players celebrated with their mothers on the pitch. Africa wept with joy.

facts.quizQuestion: Which French player scored three goals in the 2022 World Cup Final — dragging France back from 2-0 down before Argentina ultimately won on penalties?
facts.explanation: Mbappé scored twice in quick succession to level at 2-2, then scored a third in extra time to force penalties. Despite his hat-trick, France lost on penalties. He won the Golden Boot with 8 goals — the most by any player at the 2022 tournament.
facts.wowFactor: A hat-trick in a World Cup final — and still ending up on the losing side. Mbappé's 2022 final is the most extraordinary losing performance in football history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American player scored the winning penalty in the 1999 Women's World Cup Final and famously removed her jersey in celebration?
facts.explanation: Brandi Chastain scored the winning penalty against China at the Rose Bowl, Pasadena, then ripped off her jersey and fell to her knees in celebration. The photograph became one of the defining sports images of the 1990s and helped launch women's football globally.
facts.wowFactor: Brandi Chastain: the most famous sports photo of 1999, taken at the moment women's football changed forever.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the final score of the largest Women's World Cup victory in history — USA vs Thailand in 2019?
facts.explanation: The United States defeated Thailand 13-0 in the 2019 Women's World Cup group stage. Alex Morgan scored 5 goals. The match generated controversy over the American players' exuberant celebrations when Thailand's players were visibly distressed.
facts.wowFactor: 13-0. Alex Morgan scored 5. The most one-sided Women's World Cup result in history — and it sparked a global debate about sportsmanship.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American player headed in a 122nd-minute equalizer against Brazil in the 2011 Women's World Cup quarter-final — considered the greatest goal in Women's WC history?
facts.explanation: Abby Wambach headed home a Megan Rapinoe cross in the 122nd minute of the 2011 quarter-final to level at 2-2 and force a penalty shootout which the US won. The goal is the latest match-tying goal ever scored in a Women's World Cup.
facts.wowFactor: 122nd minute. USA about to go out. Rapinoe crosses. Wambach heads in. The greatest goal ever scored in the Women's World Cup.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Japanese player equalized in the 117th minute of the 2011 Women's World Cup Final, allowing Japan to ultimately win on penalties?
facts.explanation: Homare Sawa tapped in a cross to level Japan vs USA at 2-2 in the 117th minute of the 2011 final. Japan had been seconds from elimination. They won 3-1 on penalties, winning the Women's World Cup four months after the devastating Tōhoku earthquake.
facts.wowFactor: 117 minutes. Seconds from elimination. Homare Sawa tapped it in. Japan won it for the earthquake victims.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Just Fontaine set the all-time record for goals scored in a single World Cup tournament, with 13 goals?
facts.explanation: Just Fontaine's 13-goal record was set at the 1958 World Cup in Sweden. France finished third. Fontaine scored in every match including a final third-place match hat-trick. The record has stood for more than 65 years.
facts.wowFactor: 1958. Just Fontaine. 13 goals. 6 games. The record nobody will ever break.

facts.quizQuestion: How old was Pelé when he scored in the 1958 World Cup Final, making him the youngest player in history to score in a World Cup Final?
facts.explanation: Pelé was 17 years and 249 days old when he scored in Brazil's 5-2 final victory over Sweden in 1958. He scored twice in the match. He remains the youngest scorer in a World Cup Final in history.
facts.wowFactor: 17 years old. Scoring in the World Cup Final. Crying afterwards. The youngest moment of football's greatest ever story.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year was the inaugural FIFA World Cup held — in Uruguay?
facts.explanation: The first FIFA World Cup was held in Uruguay in July 1930. Only 13 teams entered — most European nations boycotted due to the cost of a transatlantic voyage. Uruguay hosted and won, defeating Argentina 4-2 in the final.
facts.wowFactor: Football's greatest competition started with 13 teams, a cross-ocean trip, and a European boycott. Not quite the spectacle it would become.

facts.quizQuestion: How many FIFA World Cup titles has Brazil won — the most of any nation in the men's tournament?
facts.explanation: Brazil has won the men's World Cup five times: 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002. They are also the only nation to have qualified for every single World Cup (all 22 tournaments from 1930 to 2022).
facts.wowFactor: Five titles. Every single World Cup ever played. Brazil IS the World Cup.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation is the only one to have qualified for all 22 FIFA World Cups ever held?
facts.explanation: Brazil is the only nation in history to have qualified for every single FIFA World Cup — all 22 men's editions from 1930 to 2022. No other nation has come close; Germany and Italy have each missed at least one tournament.
facts.wowFactor: 22 World Cups. 22 qualifications. Brazil has never watched from home. Not once in 92 years.

facts.quizQuestion: How many World Cup finals (combined) has Germany/West Germany appeared in — the most of any nation?
facts.explanation: Germany (combined as West Germany and unified Germany) has appeared in 8 World Cup Finals: 1954, 1966, 1974, 1982, 1986, 1990, 2002, and 2014. They have won 4 of them. No other nation has reached as many finals.
facts.wowFactor: 8 finals. 4 wins. Germany has been in more World Cup finals than any other nation in football history.

facts.quizQuestion: How many FIFA World Cup titles has Germany won in total — counting both West Germany and the unified German team?
facts.explanation: Germany has won four World Cups: West Germany won in 1954, 1974, and 1990; unified Germany won in 2014. The 2014 title came in Brazil and was the first won by the unified nation.
facts.wowFactor: Four titles across two eras and two national identities — Germany's World Cup story spans reunification itself.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won back-to-back World Cup titles in 1934 and 1938 — the only team to retain the trophy before WWII ended the tournament?
facts.explanation: Italy won the 1934 World Cup on home soil and retained the trophy in France in 1938, defeating Hungary 4-2. WWII then cancelled the 1942 and 1946 editions. Italy also won in 1982 and 2006 for a total of four titles.
facts.wowFactor: Italy's back-to-back wins in the 1930s — and then WWII stopped the tournament for 12 years. Football and history will always be intertwined.

facts.quizQuestion: How many consecutive World Cups did Italy fail to qualify for after winning the 2006 tournament?
facts.explanation: After winning the 2006 World Cup, Italy failed to qualify for both 2018 and 2022 — the greatest fall from grace in World Cup history. A four-time champion watching from home in consecutive tournaments shocked the football world.
facts.wowFactor: 2006 World Cup winners. Did not qualify for 2018. Did not qualify for 2022. Football is a cruel sport.

facts.quizQuestion: How many FIFA World Cup titles has Argentina won — with each title led by a different iconic player?
facts.explanation: Argentina has won three World Cups: 1978 (led by Mario Kempes), 1986 (led by Diego Maradona), and 2022 (led by Lionel Messi). Each title was defined by a player who transcended the sport in their era.
facts.wowFactor: 1978: Kempes. 1986: Maradona. 2022: Messi. Argentina's three World Cups are three chapters in football's greatest story.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation is the only defending World Cup champion to reach the final in the immediately following tournament, winning in 1998 and losing the 2022 final?
facts.explanation: France won the 1998 World Cup and won again in 2018. In 2022, they became the first defending champion since Brazil in 1998 to reach the final, losing to Argentina on penalties despite Mbappé's hat-trick.
facts.wowFactor: France reached the 2022 final as defending champions and scored 3 goals in the final — and still lost. Mbappé had scored twice in 97 seconds.

facts.quizQuestion: Which small South American nation has won the World Cup twice (1930 and 1950) and remains the smallest country by population ever to win it?
facts.explanation: Uruguay has won the World Cup twice and has a population of just 3.5 million. They won the inaugural 1930 tournament and shocked the world with the Maracanazo in 1950. They've also won 15 Copa America titles — the most in that tournament's history.
facts.wowFactor: 3.5 million people. Two World Cups. The most Copa Americas ever. Uruguay is the smallest overachiever in football history.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did England win their only FIFA World Cup title?
facts.explanation: England's only World Cup came in 1966, on home soil at Wembley. Geoff Hurst scored a hat-trick in the final as England defeated West Germany 4-2 after extra time. The tournament was the first to be broadcast in colour television in some countries.
facts.wowFactor: 1966: England's only World Cup. Every England fan born since has had to hear about it — and none of them were there.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Spain win their only FIFA World Cup title — the first European nation to win a World Cup held outside Europe?
facts.explanation: Spain won the 2010 World Cup in South Africa, defeating the Netherlands 1-0 after extra time. It was the first World Cup held on African soil, and the first time a European nation won a tournament staged outside Europe.
facts.wowFactor: Spain in 2010: Euro 2008, World Cup 2010, Euro 2012. The most dominant international football generation in history.

facts.quizQuestion: How many FIFA Women's World Cup titles has the United States won — the most of any nation?
facts.explanation: The United States has won the Women's World Cup four times: 1991, 1999, 2015, and 2019. They have played in every Women's World Cup and have won more than any other nation by a significant margin.
facts.wowFactor: Four titles. The United States built women's football — and built it to win.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won back-to-back Women's World Cup titles in 2003 and 2007, led by Birgit Prinz?
facts.explanation: Germany won consecutive Women's World Cups in 2003 and 2007, both times with Birgit Prinz as the dominant player. The 2007 tournament was particularly dominant — Germany did not concede a single goal across all 6 matches.
facts.wowFactor: Germany's 2007 Women's World Cup: 6 games, 21 goals scored, 0 conceded. Total dominance.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won the 2011 Women's World Cup four months after a devastating national earthquake and tsunami — becoming Asia's first Women's World Cup champion?
facts.explanation: Japan won the 2011 Women's World Cup, defeating the United States on penalties in the final. It was Japan's first Women's World Cup and came four months after the Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami that killed nearly 16,000 people.
facts.wowFactor: Asia's first Women's World Cup champion — won in the shadow of a national tragedy that made the victory transcend sport.

facts.quizQuestion: Which nation won the 2023 Women's World Cup, completing a remarkable year in which Aitana Bonmatí also won the Ballon d'Or Féminin?
facts.explanation: Spain won the 2023 Women's World Cup on their first-ever final appearance, defeating England 1-0 in Sydney. The triumph came despite major turmoil in the Spanish women's team — players had publicly called for their coach's removal before the tournament.
facts.wowFactor: Team mutiny before the tournament. Final on first appearance. Aitana Bonmatí wins everything. Spain's 2023 is one of football's most chaotic success stories.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country hosted the 1986 FIFA World Cup for the second time — originally awarded to Colombia before they withdrew?
facts.explanation: Mexico became the first country to host the World Cup twice in 1986, after Colombia withdrew due to economic problems. Maradona's legendary tournament took place at the iconic Estadio Azteca in Mexico City.
facts.wowFactor: Colombia was supposed to host 1986. Mexico stepped in at two years' notice — and hosted the greatest tournament ever.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country hosted the 2019 FIFA Women's World Cup, where the United States won their fourth title?
facts.explanation: France hosted the 2019 Women's World Cup. The US defeated Netherlands 2-0 in the final in Lyon. Megan Rapinoe won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot.
facts.wowFactor: France 2019: a billion viewers, the greatest US women's team ever assembled, and an equal pay movement that overshadowed the football.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country was the runner-up in the 1974 FIFA World Cup Final, losing to West Germany despite their revolutionary Total Football?
facts.explanation: The Netherlands reached the 1974 final with their revolutionary Total Football system led by Johan Cruyff. They scored before West Germany had even touched the ball, yet lost 2-1. The Dutch would reach the final again in 1978.
facts.wowFactor: Netherlands scored before West Germany touched the ball — then lost the final. Total Football conquered everything except the trophy.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country was the runner-up in the 2006 FIFA World Cup Final, despite having Zinedine Zidane sent off for a headbutt?
