facts.explanation: France reached the 2006 final where Zidane's infamous headbutt on Materazzi overshadowed the match. France lost on penalties to Italy, with the shootout decided after Zidane's expulsion in extra time.
facts.wowFactor: France reached the final with a 34-year-old Zidane coming out of international retirement. They nearly won it — then he headbutted someone.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country reached the 2018 FIFA World Cup Final for the first time, losing to France in a 4-2 thriller?
facts.explanation: Croatia reached their first World Cup Final in 2018, guided by Luka Modrić who won the Golden Ball. They beat Denmark, Russia, England, and Argentina on their way to Moscow, only to lose to France in the final.
facts.wowFactor: Croatia — population 4 million — reached the World Cup Final. A small nation, a brilliant midfield, an inspirational captain.

facts.quizQuestion: Which host nation was the runner-up (in the final round-robin) at the 1950 World Cup — suffering the famous 'Maracanazo' defeat to Uruguay?
facts.explanation: Brazil only needed a draw in the final round-robin match to win the 1950 World Cup but lost 2-1 to Uruguay before 173,850 fans at the Maracanã. The defeat became known as the Maracanazo and haunted Brazilian football for generations.
facts.wowFactor: Brazil needed a draw. They had 173,850 fans. They lost 2-1. The Maracanazo is the most traumatic moment in Brazilian sporting history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian player scored twice in the 2002 World Cup Final to win the Golden Boot, completing one of football's greatest personal redemptions?
facts.explanation: Ronaldo (Brazil) scored both goals in Brazil's 2-0 final win over Germany. Four years earlier, he had collapsed mysteriously the night before the 1998 final. His 2002 triumph — complete with the double-arc haircut — is one of sport's most extraordinary redemption stories.
facts.wowFactor: Seizure in 1998. Mystery before the final. Played anyway. Lost. Then 2002: two goals in the final. Ronaldo's journey is football's greatest comeback.

facts.quizQuestion: Which player scored Germany's 7th goal against Brazil in the 2014 semi-final — breaking the all-time World Cup scoring record in the process?
facts.explanation: Miroslav Klose scored Germany's 7th goal in the 7-1 demolition of Brazil, taking his career total to 16 goals and passing Ronaldo (Brazil)'s record of 15. He broke the record in Brazil, against Brazil — one of sport's most poetic moments.
facts.wowFactor: He broke the all-time World Cup scoring record by scoring Germany's 7th goal against Brazil — in Brazil. The stadium was already silent.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian player won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot at the 2007 Women's World Cup — the highest-profile individual performance of her era?
facts.explanation: Marta won the Golden Ball and Golden Boot (7 goals) at the 2007 Women's World Cup in China, despite Brazil losing the final to Germany. She was widely regarded as the world's best female player throughout the 2006-2012 period.
facts.wowFactor: The best player in the world — and her team still lost. Marta's 2007 is women's football's most brilliant individual performance without a winner's medal.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American player won the 2019 Women's World Cup Golden Ball and was equally known for her outspoken advocacy for equal pay?
facts.explanation: Megan Rapinoe won the Golden Ball and Golden Boot at France 2019 while becoming a global symbol for gender equality in sports. The US team's equal pay lawsuit against US Soccer became as much a story as the World Cup itself.
facts.wowFactor: Rapinoe won the World Cup and then sued her own federation for equal pay. She won both.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Germany retain the Women's World Cup in China, with Marta winning both Golden Ball and Boot but still losing the final?
facts.explanation: The 2007 Women's World Cup was held in China (the country that was originally scheduled to host 2003 before SARS). Germany won the title without conceding a goal across all 6 matches — the most dominant run in Women's World Cup history.
facts.wowFactor: Germany 2007: 6 games, 21 goals scored, 0 conceded. The most complete Women's World Cup campaign ever.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Carli Lloyd score a hat-trick in 16 minutes to give the United States a 5-2 Women's World Cup Final victory over Japan in Canada?
facts.explanation: The 2015 Women's World Cup Final in Vancouver saw the US obliterate Japan 5-2, with Carli Lloyd scoring three goals in the opening 16 minutes. Her lob from the halfway line made it 4-0 before halftime.
facts.wowFactor: 2015: USA 5-2 Japan in the final. Hat-trick in 16 minutes. Goal from the halfway line. The tournament's greatest ever single-player final performance.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did the United States win their fourth Women's World Cup in France — setting a world record of 1 billion viewers?
facts.explanation: The 2019 Women's World Cup in France was the most-watched Women's World Cup in history with approximately 1 billion viewers. USA defeated Netherlands 2-0 in the final in Lyon. Megan Rapinoe won the Golden Ball and Boot.
facts.wowFactor: 1 billion viewers for the 2019 Women's World Cup final. Women's football had truly gone global.

facts.quizQuestion: In which stadium were BOTH the 1970 World Cup Final (Brazil 4-1 Italy) and the 1986 World Cup Final (Argentina 3-2 West Germany) played?
facts.explanation: The Estadio Azteca in Mexico City hosted World Cup finals in both 1970 and 1986 — the only stadium to have hosted two World Cup finals. It was also the setting for Maradona's Hand of God and Goal of the Century in 1986.
facts.wowFactor: The same stadium hosted Brazil in 1970 AND Maradona in 1986. The Estadio Azteca has seen more World Cup magic than any other ground.

facts.quizQuestion: In which stadium was the 2022 World Cup Final played — the greatest final in history between Argentina and France?
facts.explanation: Lusail Stadium in Qatar hosted the 2022 World Cup Final, the most-watched sporting event in history (3.572 billion viewers). Argentina defeated France 4-2 on penalties after a 3-3 draw.
facts.wowFactor: Lusail Stadium: 88,966 people watched the greatest World Cup final ever played. Messi lifted the trophy he had spent 20 years dreaming of.

facts.quizQuestion: In which stadium was the 1994 World Cup Final played — the same venue where Brandi Chastain scored her famous penalty five years later?
facts.explanation: The Rose Bowl in Pasadena hosted the 1994 Men's World Cup Final (Brazil vs Italy, 0-0, 3-2 pens) and the 1999 Women's World Cup Final (USA vs China, 0-0, 5-4 pens). It is the only stadium to have hosted both finals.
facts.wowFactor: The Rose Bowl hosted the 1994 Men's WC Final AND the 1999 Women's WC Final. Baggio's miss and Chastain's shirt in the same stadium.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Chinese player shared the 1999 Women's World Cup Golden Boot with Brazil's Sissi, both scoring 7 goals?
facts.explanation: Sun Wen and Sissi (Brazil) both scored 7 goals at the 1999 Women's World Cup — the only time two players have tied for the Golden Boot in the tournament's history. Sun Wen also won the Golden Ball despite China losing the final.
facts.wowFactor: Tied for top scorer AND won the best player award — all in a tournament China lost on penalties. Sun Wen's 1999 is women's football's most overlooked masterclass.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian player won the 2007 Women's World Cup Golden Boot with 7 goals — her second consecutive tournament as top scorer?
facts.explanation: Marta won the Golden Boot at the 2007 Women's World Cup with 7 goals, adding to the Golden Ball and Boot she won at the same tournament. It was part of her unprecedented run of individual excellence in the 2006-2010 period.
facts.wowFactor: Back-to-back Golden Boots. Seven goals each time. Marta was so dominant that the award became almost procedural.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American player won the 2019 Women's World Cup Golden Boot with 6 goals — also winning the Golden Ball in the same tournament?
facts.explanation: Megan Rapinoe won the 2019 Golden Boot with 6 goals and the Golden Ball. She famously stated before a match she would not visit the White House, creating international headlines. Despite the controversy, she was the tournament's defining player.
facts.wowFactor: Rapinoe won the Golden Boot and the Golden Ball. Also made the US political establishment uncomfortable. A complete tournament in every sense.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the famous scoreline when Germany demolished the host nation in the 2014 World Cup semi-final — the 'Mineirazo'?
facts.explanation: Germany defeated Brazil 7-1 at the Estádio Mineirão on July 8, 2014. Germany scored 5 goals in 18 minutes in the second half, reducing Brazil's players and fans to tears. Brazil were without both Neymar (injured) and Thiago Silva (suspended).
facts.wowFactor: 7-1. On Brazilian soil. Germany scored 5 in 18 minutes. Brazil's captain and star player were both absent. The perfect storm of humiliation.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the record-breaking scoreline when the United States set the all-time Women's World Cup victory margin in the 2019 group stage?
facts.explanation: The United States defeated Thailand 13-0 in the 2019 Women's World Cup group stage — the largest margin of victory in Women's World Cup history. Alex Morgan scored 5 goals. The match generated controversy over the US team's celebratory behavior.
facts.wowFactor: 13-0. The biggest winning margin in Women's World Cup history — and it sparked a global debate about whether scoring keeps going when you're already winning big.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the scoreline of the Men's World Cup record victory — Hungary's demolition of El Salvador in the 1982 group stage?
facts.explanation: Hungary defeated El Salvador 10-1 in the 1982 World Cup group stage — the highest-scoring match in Men's World Cup history. László Kiss scored a hat-trick as a substitute. Despite this result, Hungary failed to advance from the group.
facts.wowFactor: 10-1 — the Men's World Cup record win. Hungary then failed to advance from the group. The biggest win in WC history and they still went home early.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the scoreline of the 1958 World Cup Final — Pelé's debut final, in which a 17-year-old scored twice?
facts.explanation: Brazil defeated Sweden 5-2 at Råsunda Stadium in 1958. Pelé scored twice in the final, becoming the youngest goalscorer in a World Cup Final at 17 years and 249 days. Vavá also scored twice.
facts.wowFactor: Brazil 5-2 Sweden. A 17-year-old named Pelé scored twice. The world's greatest player had arrived — and nobody was ready.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the scoreline when France shocked Brazil in the 1998 World Cup Final — Zidane heading twice before Brazil's star Ronaldo (Brazil) played through a health crisis?
facts.explanation: France defeated Brazil 3-0 in Paris, with Zidane heading in twice and Emmanuel Petit adding a third. Brazil's Ronaldo (Brazil) played despite a mysterious seizure the night before the match. France's first World Cup title.
facts.wowFactor: France 3-0 Brazil. Zidane: two headers. Ronaldo (Brazil) barely present after a mysterious night. France's first World Cup, and nobody saw it coming.

facts.quizQuestion: Against which team did a 17-year-old Brazilian forward score a hat-trick in the 1958 World Cup semifinal — becoming the youngest player ever to do so?
facts.explanation: Pelé scored three goals against France in the 1958 semifinal at the age of 17 years and 244 days — a record for the youngest hat-trick scorer in World Cup history. Brazil went on to win the 1958 final against Sweden, giving Pelé his first of three titles.
facts.wowFactor: Seventeen years old, hat-trick in the World Cup semifinal — Pelé announced himself to the planet in a single match.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Brazil beat Italy 4-1 in a World Cup Final where Pelé opened the scoring with a header and Carlos Alberto closed with a thunderbolt — a match often called the greatest final of all time?
facts.explanation: The 1970 World Cup Final saw Brazil beat Italy 4-1 in Mexico City. Pelé headed in the opening goal, and Carlos Alberto's fourth goal — a rocket from the edge of the box following a 10-touch team move — is considered one of the greatest goals ever scored.
facts.wowFactor: The 1970 final: Pelé headed the first, Carlos Alberto thunderbolted the fourth. Italy were helpless.

facts.quizQuestion: Diego Maradona scored both the 'Hand of God' goal and the 'Goal of the Century' in the same 1986 World Cup quarterfinal match — against which country?
facts.explanation: In the 1986 quarterfinal against England, Maradona first punched the ball into the net with his hand (claiming it was 'the hand of God') and then dribbled past five players and the goalkeeper for what FIFA voters later chose as the Goal of the Century. Argentina won 2-1 and went on to win the tournament.
facts.wowFactor: In one match against England, Maradona committed the most notorious cheat in football history AND produced the greatest individual goal ever scored.

facts.quizQuestion: Diego Maradona captained Argentina to the 1990 World Cup Final, which they lost 1-0. Which country defeated them in that final?
facts.explanation: West Germany beat Argentina 1-0 in the 1990 final in Rome — a rematch of the 1986 final that Argentina had won. A controversial late penalty decided the match. Maradona wept on the pitch, leaving with a suspension that would haunt the end of his international career.
facts.wowFactor: 1986 final: Argentina beat West Germany. 1990 final: West Germany beat Argentina. The greatest rematch in World Cup history.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Diego Maradona score across his four World Cup tournaments (1982, 1986, 1990, 1994)?
facts.explanation: Maradona scored 8 goals in 21 World Cup matches. While not the highest tally, his combination of goals, assists, and individual match-winning performances in 1986 make him the most impactful single player in World Cup history by many accounts.
facts.wowFactor: 8 goals in 21 matches — but one of those 8 was possibly the most famous ever scored, and another was possibly the most notorious.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Argentine player became the youngest goal scorer in his country's World Cup history when he scored against Serbia and Montenegro at the 2006 World Cup at age 18?
facts.explanation: At 18 years and 357 days old, Messi came off the bench to score Argentina's sixth goal against Serbia and Montenegro in 2006 — the youngest Argentine to score at a World Cup. It was a hint of what was to come across five more tournaments.
facts.wowFactor: Youngest Argentine scorer at a World Cup — and 16 years later, he'd finally lift the trophy.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Lionel Messi win a controversial World Cup Golden Ball despite Argentina losing the final to Germany — a decision many felt was politically motivated?
facts.explanation: Messi won the 2014 Golden Ball after scoring 4 goals and assisting in Argentina's run to the final, which they lost 1-0 to Germany in extra time. The award was widely debated, with many arguing Germany's Manuel Neuer or Götze deserved it more. Messi himself admitted it felt hollow without the trophy.
facts.wowFactor: The Golden Ball without the gold trophy — Messi's 2014 win sparked one of football's most enduring debates.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals has Lionel Messi scored across his five World Cup tournaments — the most of any Argentine player in history?
facts.explanation: Messi scored 13 World Cup goals across 2006, 2010, 2014, 2018, and 2022, including 7 goals and 3 assists in the 2022 tournament alone. His 2022 campaign, capped with the title, is widely considered the greatest individual World Cup performance by any player.
facts.wowFactor: 13 World Cup goals across five tournaments — the last one finally came with a winner's medal.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Portuguese striker became the first man to score at five different FIFA World Cups, achieving the feat at Qatar 2022 at age 37?
facts.explanation: Cristiano Ronaldo scored in 2006, 2010, 2014, 2018, and 2022, making him the only man to score in five different World Cup tournaments. He shares this record with no one else. Portugal reached the quarterfinals in 2022 without him scoring in the knockout stages.
facts.wowFactor: Five World Cups, five scored — no man has ever done that before.

facts.quizQuestion: Against which World Cup opponent did Cristiano Ronaldo score a hat-trick in the 2018 group stage — including a stunning last-minute free kick to draw 3-3?
facts.explanation: In a breathtaking 3-3 draw with Spain at the 2018 World Cup, Cristiano Ronaldo scored all three of Portugal's goals, including a stoppage-time free kick into the top corner. It remains one of the most dramatic individual performances in World Cup group stage history.
facts.wowFactor: 3-3 vs Spain in a World Cup group game, all three goals from the same man, the last one in stoppage time. Pure theatre.

facts.quizQuestion: How many World Cup goals has Cristiano Ronaldo scored across his five tournaments with Portugal?
facts.explanation: Cristiano Ronaldo scored 8 World Cup goals across 2006, 2010, 2014, 2018, and 2022 — with all of those goals coming in the group stage or early rounds, as Portugal never reached a final. His World Cup record contrasts with his domestic tally of 900+ career club and international goals.
facts.wowFactor: 8 World Cup goals across 5 tournaments — all group-stage or early round, no knockout goals in his entire World Cup career.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Brazilian striker suffered a mysterious pre-match convulsive fit on the day of the 1998 World Cup Final, was initially left out of the lineup, and then reportedly re-added hours before kickoff — as France won 3-0?
facts.explanation: The 1998 final is haunted by what happened before it. Ronaldo (Brazil) had a convulsive episode at the team hotel on the day of the match and was initially left off the team sheet, then restored hours later. He played but was clearly unwell; France won 3-0. The truth of what happened has never been fully explained.
facts.wowFactor: The greatest striker in the world collapses the morning of the World Cup Final — and nobody has ever fully explained why.

facts.quizQuestion: Zinedine Zidane scored two headed goals in the first half of the 1998 World Cup Final as France won 3-0 — against which opponent?
facts.explanation: Zidane headed two corners into the net from Youri Djorkaeff deliveries in the first half to give France a 2-0 lead against Brazil at the Stade de France. Emmanuel Petit added a third late on. It remains France's greatest footballing night and launched Zidane into global superstardom.
facts.wowFactor: Two headed goals from a midfielder in a World Cup final — against Brazil — in front of your home crowd.

facts.quizQuestion: Which French midfielder was sent off in the final minutes of the 2006 World Cup Final for headbutting Italian defender Marco Materazzi in the chest — in his final professional match?
facts.explanation: Zinedine Zidane's headbutt on Marco Materazzi in the 109th minute of extra time in the 2006 final was his last ever act as a professional footballer. He received a red card and walked past the World Cup trophy as France went on to lose the final on penalties to Italy. Materazzi later admitted he had taunted Zidane with an insult about his sister.
facts.wowFactor: He walked past the World Cup trophy on his way off the pitch after the red card. His last act in football. Ever.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Zinedine Zidane represent at the FIFA World Cup — winning it in 1998 and reaching the final again in 2006?
facts.explanation: Born in Marseille to Algerian parents, Zidane chose to represent France and became one of the greatest players in French football history. His 1998 brace in the final and his 2006 Golden Ball (despite the red card) bookend a remarkable World Cup career that earned two final appearances.
facts.wowFactor: Son of Algerian immigrants, winner for France, Golden Ball in his final match — Zidane's story is as complex as his football.

facts.quizQuestion: Who became only the second teenager to score in a World Cup Final, netting for France in the 2018 final at age 19 — 60 years after Pelé set the original record?
facts.explanation: Kylian Mbappé scored in France's 4-2 win over Croatia in the 2018 final at age 19, becoming only the second teenager ever to score in a World Cup Final. Pelé was the first, scoring at age 17 in the 1958 final — 60 years earlier.
facts.wowFactor: Only the second teenager in World Cup final history to score — the first was Pelé, 60 years earlier.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Kylian Mbappé score in the 2022 World Cup Final — including a late brace that forced extra time before Argentina won on penalties?
facts.explanation: Mbappé scored a hat-trick in the 2022 final, becoming only the second player ever to do so (after Geoff Hurst in 1966). With France trailing 3-1 with 10 minutes left, he scored twice to level at 3-3, then scored again in extra time before Argentina won the penalty shootout. It was the greatest World Cup final in modern history.
facts.wowFactor: A hat-trick in a losing final. 3-1 down with 10 minutes left. The greatest World Cup final comeback that still ended in defeat.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Just Fontaine play his only World Cup — scoring 13 goals in 6 games to set an all-time single-tournament record that still stands?
facts.explanation: Fontaine played only one World Cup — 1958 in Sweden — and scored 13 goals in 6 games. A knee injury ended his career before the next tournament. His record of 13 goals in a single World Cup remains unbroken over 65 years later and is considered one of the most unbreakable records in football.
facts.wowFactor: One World Cup, 13 goals, one knee injury — and that record has stood for over 65 years.

facts.quizQuestion: Which West German striker scored the winning goal in the 1974 World Cup Final against the Netherlands — his last act in international football before announcing his retirement?
facts.explanation: Gerd Müller scored the 43rd-minute winner in the 1974 final against the Netherlands, giving West Germany a 2-1 victory. It was the last goal of his international career — he announced his retirement from the national team immediately after, having scored 14 World Cup goals and 68 international goals in total.
facts.wowFactor: His last ever international goal was the World Cup winner. Then he walked away. Job done.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Gerd Müller — the third-highest World Cup scorer of all time — represent when he won the 1974 World Cup?
facts.explanation: Gerd Müller represented West Germany (not Germany) at two World Cups — 1970 and 1974 — scoring 14 goals across both tournaments. The German reunification in 1990 means all pre-1991 German players represented 'West Germany', a distinction important for football records.
facts.wowFactor: Third on the all-time scorer list — and both his World Cups were for West Germany, a country that no longer exists.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Miroslav Klose win his only World Cup title with Germany — at age 36, becoming a champion alongside the generation he had inspired?
facts.explanation: Klose won the 2014 World Cup with Germany in Brazil, aged 36, after three previous tournaments (2002, 2006, 2010). He scored 1 goal in 2014 to bring his all-time record to 16. The 2014 semifinal 7-1 win over Brazil — the Mineirazo — happened with Klose on the pitch.
facts.wowFactor: Four World Cups, 16 goals, finally a winner's medal — at age 36 in enemy territory (Brazil).

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Franz Beckenbauer captain West Germany to the World Cup title — 16 years before he would manage the same country to another title in 1990?
facts.explanation: Beckenbauer captained West Germany to the 1974 World Cup title on home soil, defeating the Netherlands 2-1 in the final. In 1990, as manager, he led Germany to a third title against Argentina. He remains one of only two people ever to win the World Cup as both captain and coach; the other is Mario Zagallo of Brazil.
facts.wowFactor: Captain in 1974, manager in 1990 — 16 years apart. The only German to win the World Cup in both roles.

facts.quizQuestion: Mario Zagallo is the only person in football history to win the World Cup in four different roles — player (1958, 1962), head coach (1970), and assistant coach (1994). Which country did he serve throughout?
facts.explanation: Mario Zagallo's four World Cup titles as player (1958, 1962), head coach (1970), and assistant (1994) with Brazil is unmatched in football history. Beckenbauer won as player and coach (2 roles); Zagallo won in 4 distinct roles across 36 years, all with the same nation.
facts.wowFactor: Four World Cups, four roles (player, player, head coach, assistant), all with Brazil. No one else comes close.

facts.quizQuestion: How many goals did Marta score at the 2007 Women's World Cup — winning both the Golden Ball (best player) and Silver Boot (second-top scorer)?
facts.explanation: Marta scored 7 goals at the 2007 Women's World Cup in China, winning the Golden Ball as best player and the Silver Boot as second-top scorer (Germany's Birgit Prinz scored the same). Brazil reached the final but lost to Germany. Marta's 2007 tournament is widely considered the greatest individual performance in Women's World Cup history.
facts.wowFactor: 7 goals, Golden Ball, Silver Boot — yet her team still lost the final. The greatest individual performance that came up just short.

facts.quizQuestion: How old was Marta when she became the oldest goalscorer in Women's World Cup history at the 2019 tournament in France?
facts.explanation: Marta scored against Italy at the 2019 Women's World Cup at age 33 years and 133 days, becoming the oldest scorer in Women's World Cup history at the time. That goal was her 17th career Women's World Cup goal — the all-time record. Brazil still lost in the round of 16.
facts.wowFactor: 33 years old, 17th World Cup goal, oldest scorer ever — Brazil still went out in the round of 16. The record came in defeat.

facts.quizQuestion: Which American striker scored in the 122nd minute of the 2011 Women's World Cup quarterfinal against Brazil — one of the most dramatic headers in the history of the sport — to force a penalty shootout the USA eventually won?
facts.explanation: With the USA reduced to 10 players and down to their last moments against Brazil in the 2011 quarterfinal, Megan Rapinoe crossed from the left and Wambach headed in from close range in the 122nd minute. The USA then won on penalties and eventually finished as runners-up. ESPN voted it the greatest women's sports moment of the decade.
facts.wowFactor: 122nd minute, 10 players vs 11, Brazil, and the header that kept the dream alive. Sports doesn't get more dramatic.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Abby Wambach win the Women's World Cup with the USA — her first and only title, in her final tournament?
facts.explanation: Wambach won the 2015 Women's World Cup with the USA in Canada, in what proved to be her final World Cup tournament. The USA beat Japan 5-2 in the final, with Carli Lloyd scoring a hat-trick. Wambach retired shortly after, holding the all-time international goals record (184) for both men and women.
facts.wowFactor: Four World Cups, one title — won in her final tournament, alongside the Carli Lloyd hat-trick that made the 2015 final famous.

facts.quizQuestion: How many Women's World Cup goals did Birgit Prinz score in her career — the record she held until Marta broke it?
facts.explanation: Birgit Prinz scored 14 goals in Women's World Cup tournaments from 1995 to 2011, holding the all-time record until Marta surpassed her in 2007. Prinz also won the FIFA Women's World Player of the Year award three consecutive times (2003, 2004, 2005) and won the World Cup with Germany in 2003 and 2007.
facts.wowFactor: 14 goals, two World Cup titles, three FIFA Player of the Year awards — then Marta showed up.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Women's World Cup legend Birgit Prinz represent — winning consecutive World Cup titles in 2003 and 2007?
facts.explanation: Birgit Prinz represented Germany throughout a career spanning five World Cups (1995–2011), winning back-to-back titles in 2003 (vs Sweden) and 2007 (vs Brazil). She is considered the greatest European player in Women's World Cup history and one of the most decorated players globally.
facts.wowFactor: Back-to-back Women's World Cup titles — Germany's most decorated player in women's football history.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Carli Lloyd win the Women's World Cup Golden Ball — the same year she scored a hat-trick in the final, including a goal from the halfway line?
facts.explanation: Carli Lloyd won the 2015 Women's World Cup Golden Ball after scoring 3 goals in the final against Japan in 16 minutes — the third was a stunning strike from the halfway line that left the Japanese goalkeeper stranded. The USA won 5-2. Lloyd also scored a Golden Ball-winning brace in the 2008 Olympics final, making her one of the most clutch big-game players in women's football history.
facts.wowFactor: Hat-trick in 16 minutes in a World Cup final — the third from the halfway line. It was the biggest stage and she was at her absolute best.

facts.quizQuestion: Against which country did Carli Lloyd score her famous 2015 Women's World Cup Final hat-trick — the opponents who had defeated the USA in the 2011 final?
facts.explanation: Japan defeated the USA in the 2011 Women's World Cup Final on penalties. The 2015 rematch in the final saw the USA win 5-2, with Carli Lloyd scoring 3 in the first 16 minutes. The result was seen as revenge for 2011 and a statement by the USA that they had reclaimed their dominance of the women's game.
facts.wowFactor: Japan beat them in 2011 — the USA came back four years later and scored 5 in the final. Lloyd scored 3 of them.

facts.quizQuestion: In which year did Homare Sawa captain Japan to their first Women's World Cup title — in a tournament where she won both the Golden Ball and Golden Boot?
facts.explanation: Sawa captained Japan to their first and only Women's World Cup title in 2011, defeating the USA on penalties. She won both the Golden Ball (best player) and Golden Boot (top scorer with 5 goals), becoming the first Asian player to win the Women's World Cup Golden Ball. Japan dedicated the victory to victims of the Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami earlier that year.
facts.wowFactor: Captain, Golden Ball, Golden Boot — and the title dedicated to earthquake survivors. Japan's greatest sporting moment.

facts.quizQuestion: Which country did Homare Sawa represent — becoming the first player from her nation to win the Women's World Cup Golden Ball in 2011?
facts.explanation: Homare Sawa represented Japan in six Women's World Cups (1995–2015), a record for any player at the time. Her 2011 Golden Ball win made her the first Asian player to receive the award in Women's World Cup history. She retired in 2015 as Japan's most capped player.
facts.wowFactor: Six World Cups, the 2011 Golden Ball and Golden Boot — the first Asian player to win the Women's World Cup award.
